title: Stop Using OpenCode
excerpt: If you don’t know what OpenCode is, imagine a boot stamping on a human face forever. The boot is made of TypeScript and the face is everything we have learned about security and systems software since the invention of the electronic computer in the 1940s. The creators describe it as an AI coding agent. As far as I can tell it’s the most popular open-source coding agent, and it currently has 161k stars on GitHub.
content_html:
If you don’t know what OpenCode is, imagine a boot stamping on a human face forever. The boot is made of TypeScript and the face is everything we have learned about security and systems software since the invention of the electronic computer in the 1940s. The creators describe it as an AI coding agent. As far as I can tell it’s the most popular open-source coding agent, and it currently has 161k stars on GitHub.

I’ve tried out OpenCode with a local LLM. My conclusion is that OpenCode is clown-car turboslop with a security posture of “let me bend over for you daddy”. Everyone using it should stop using it.

There are two parts to this post: annoying things and alarming things. The second part is longer. I wrote this post with reference to source code from OpenCode git version baef5cd4.

I don’t consider anything in this post to be a security disclosure. OpenCode is fundamentally a web-stack tool for piping llm | bash, and all the issues I describe are in the “pipe” part. The ways it fails are fascinating in the fractal nature of the poor decision making, but the outcome was foregone.

I tried to keep discussion of LLM use separate from whether everyone using LLMs should have their machines trivially exploited or accidentally wiped. There is a post-script with some brief thoughts on local LLMs.

Annoying Things

Let’s put security to one side for a moment and examine how OpenCode fails as a tool even when it’s not causing you to get your shit popped. There is a kind of Bethesda Effect with OpenCode where it’s impossible to tell what is a bug and what is by design, so I stuck with a description of “annoying”.

Prompt Cache Misses

Most local LLM servers use some variant of the OpenAI /v1/chat/completions API. The idea is:

  • You POST a JSON blob with the entire conversation so far.

  • You get back a stream of SSE events which add up to the response.

The upload cost over a session is quadratic, and download is amplified by wrapping tiny deltas in JSON with repeated metadata. Tool calls use the elusive “double JSON encoding” so they can be serialised as multiple JSON-encoded deltas that reassemble into more JSON.

The setup has one benefit, which is the server is stateless. As usual, the way you make stateless things fast is: state. The server caches evaluations. When it receives a request, it:

  • Finds the longest matching cached prefix.

  • Evaluates from end of prefix to end of the last posted message (“prefill”).

  • Generates new tokens until it encounters an end-of-sequence token.

I used Qwen3.6-27B dense on an M4 Max, which has decent memory bandwidth (~0.5 TB/s, high for a CPU SoC, low for a GPU). Token generation is usable but it is extremely compute-bound in prefill. If my server can’t find a good matching prefix for the request prompt when I’m deep into the window then I might have to wait 10 minutes of max GPU usage for it to start generating a response. That’s fine, because this should happen rarely. Should.

Here are some of the ways OpenCode missed the memo on this one:

  • It globs your filesystem and re-reads AGENTS.md (injected in turn-0 system prompt) on every SSE turn. If you put a quick note in AGENTS.md to be read in the next session, you immediately force a full re-evaluation.

  • It prunes context from tool calls on every agent → user transition, invalidating a large part of the prefix.

    • Pruning just discards tool call results more than a fixed distance const PRUNE_PROTECT = 40_000 behind the write head. In the best case you’re taking a 40k context miss, which is equivalent to reading a full-length novel per two or three turns.

    • Agent → user transition includes interruption, so if you need to pull the clanker out of a rabbit hole and re-steer it, OpenCode immediately trashes the prompt cache and makes you wait for a response.

  • Personal favourite: it puts the current date in the turn-0 system prompt and re-evaluates every SSE turn. If you’re using OpenCode at midnight you get a full prompt cache miss.

    • I think this tries to remediate the clanker tendency to believe the current date is the date of its training cutoff, and refuse to believe newer things exist. It’s still funny.

These are the prompt cache misses that fit solely in this category. There are many more; I’ll call them out as we go.

Pruning

I mentioned pruning in the previous section. The prompt cache misses aren’t worth it so I disabled it. The other glaring issue is the lack of protection for early reads. It might not be obvious how completely broken this is, so let’s work through an example. Suppose you start a fresh session, and tell your clanker to first read a spec or implementation plan, then write some code:

  • The spec is read into context.

  • The clanker goes and reads related code, very likely putting it over the fixed 40k pruning threshold.

  • The clanker is ready to implement, but either immediately dives down a dumb rabbit hole, or sits in chain-of-thought dithering about something that is actually very simple or already well-specified.

  • You interrupt the clanker to re-steer it.

  • The interruption causes the entire spec to be deleted from the context window.

  • The clanker writes code without being able to refer to the spec.

Pruning applies equally to all results of all tools except for skill, which is never pruned.

Compaction

Want to sit for 10 minutes while the LLM server prefills the entire session with a new prompt prefixed to it, just to turn it into 5 bullet points that go at the top of a new session? Me neither. I get what they are going for, but I’ve not seen it work well. Neither compaction nor pruning is implemented well, and they interact poorly.

If you want to summarise a session then the summarisation prompt should be injected at the end to avoid prefilling the entire session from scratch. The best method I’ve found is just an explicit handoff by telling the clanker to write out notes. It’s ugly but it works better than OpenCode’s compaction mechanism, and creates an on-disk artefact that I can edit or reuse in multiple sessions.

Compaction is a leaky abstraction that tries to make a finite context window look like an infinite one. It’s better to accept context windows and prompt caches as a first-class feature of clanker wrangling, and expose better primitives for managing them. Pi has an interesting approach here with session trees, which deliberately exploit the prompt cache.

System Prompts

OpenCode pastes a system prompt at the top of new context windows. Fine and normal, but:

  • The default system prompt is incredibly verbose. Ironically most of the word count is explaining to the LLM how to be concise.

  • The default system prompt is opinionated (fine) but it has shit opinions (not fine). It took me a while to figure out why my agent kept saying “Use ABSOLUTELY NO COMMENTS” when dispatching subagents.

  • The Plan-to-Build handover is clumsy and often leaves me towards the end of a context window by the time everything is sufficiently elucidated. I’d rather write out notes with everything discussed, so I can edit it and then hand off to a fresh session. To which, see next point:

  • The system reminder for Plan mode tells the clanker it can’t write to any directories, but it’s actually allowed to write to a specific .opencode/plans directory. I have seen this fail both ways: writing to this directory unprompted, and refusing to write when explicitly instructed.

  • There is no way to modify the default system prompt globally; you have to copy it into every project.

  • If you only override the default prompt in Build mode, switching to Plan mode is a full prompt cache miss.

Different per-model prompts have wildly varying contents and quality. They’re all worth a good hate-scroll but Beast Mode (GPT-4, o1 and o3) is my favourite. Quote:

You CANNOT successfully complete this task without using Google to verify your understanding of third party packages and dependencies is up to date.

You cannot. It’s just impossible. We don’t know how. Definitely don’t just read the source code for the package.

Permission Prompts

When the clanker tries to access a file outside of the project directory, if it does so in a way that OpenCode manages to recognise with ad-hoc string parsing (oops that is the alarming things section), you receive a prompt asking you whether to grant permission. This halts execution until you respond.

The answers are: Yes/No/Always. Do you see a missing answer here? How about Never?

The interaction with subagents here is particularly broken. If a subagent tries to access a script output in /tmp, and I say No, it kills the subagent and all of its context for its partially complete work is lost. So I have to say Yes and let it write to /tmp or whatever it’s trying to do.

The other issue is decision fatigue: if I keep getting asked “can I do this?” and the only response that leads to productivity is “yes” then I’m eventually going to nod through something dangerous. Human fallibility should not be load-bearing for something as basic as “don’t write outside this directory”.

Agent Interaction

This is feature number 0 for a coding agent. It’s broken.

  • If I send a message while SSE streaming is ongoing, it gets queued. Nice feature. However:

    • The semantics of when OpenCode decides to actually send the message are a little unclear; the code suggests it’s at the end of a tool call turn but I have also seen tool → CoT transitions without sending my queued message.

    • If I subsequently interrupt because I want the clanker to actually answer my question instead of navel gazing, the message is removed from the “queued” state and just goes into the message log. You interrupted to get the clanker to answer your message, but now you can’t send the message. You have to send a second message to start a new stream.

  • Undoing a message often fails to remove it from the message log.

The problems continue with subagents (i.e. agents spawned by an agent using a tool call RPC):

  • I can’t talk to subagents; if they go down a dumb rabbit hole I have a choice of killing them and losing their context, or helplessly watching them burn tokens.

    • I looked into this and apparently OpenCode used to have this feature but it’s just… gone?

    • You can @mention a subagent from the main agent’s chat window but this doesn’t seem to do anything useful. In particular it doesn’t interrupt.

  • If a subagent fails a tool call (e.g. Qwen putting tool calls in CoT) then it’s fatal and all the context up to that point is lost.

  • The ability to reuse subagents seems nice in theory but in practice the main use of subagents is to break tasks into smaller context windows. Having the agent sometimes decide to reuse the same subagent for an unrelated task defeats this.

    • I have never seen this be beneficial and often seen it cause prompt cache misses (there it is again!) due to switching between agents and subagents that both have huge contexts.

    • As a principle: it’s ok to have a richer interaction model from the human side, but clankers will do every possible dumb thing, so choices need to be minimised.

On the positive side, OpenCode’s subagent interactions led to one of the funniest GitHub issues I have ever had the pleasure of reading.

Agent Tools

These are the RPCs that OpenCode exposes to the LLM to access files and run commands on your machine. They made some interesting choices:

  • The edit tool uses exact search-replace, requiring unique match by default.

    • This is a good fit for clankers, as they can recall file contents precisely but aren’t always sure exactly where in the file it is as line numbers drift over multiple edits.

    • There is an option to do global search; every time I have seen a clanker use this it has been an absolute shitshow that required multiple edits to correct. Removing this option would give you exactly the Pi edit tool design.

  • The question tool in Plan mode (multi-choice) is strictly worse than just telling the LLM to ask me questions in natural language in the system prompt.

  • The grep and glob tools are redundant with bash.

    • They may be more ergonomic to use, but given I regularly see the clanker just running grep or rg in bash, I doubt it.

    • I suspect the reason is to allow read-only agent types like Explore to be restricted from bash commands.

    • This is essentially a self-admission that it’s impossible to reason about side effects of bash commands without running them. More on this later.

  • The todo tool is probably net-useful but the clanker forgets to check the TODOs. Ironic… he could save others from death but not himself.

TUI

Text UIs (what the kids call programs like GNU nano) are fashionable these days. OpenCode has one too:

  • It uses a gigabyte of RAM to render text.

  • It’s impossible to type a newline in the message box. Shift-enter is supposed to do this, it’s just broken for me.

    • I found a pre-existing issue; the developer literally said “works on my machine.” It’s closed.

  • Sometimes when typing a multiline message (i.e. too long for a single line, so gets softwrapped), the message box scrolls and the cursor continues to move, but the characters I’m typing don’t appear on the new line.

  • Trying to select text while streaming: the view autoscrolls and you lose the selection.

  • ^C closes the session instantly. This isn’t how interactive shells are supposed to work. ^C should interrupt a currently running command, and ^D should close the session if a command is not currently running.

  • Text box does not respond to normal shortcuts (e.g. Option+right/left to go forward/back word on Mac).

  • Markdown re-render (or something else, I didn’t profile it) takes multiple seconds to stream new chunks once a message or CoT is long. Someone did a quadratic fucky-wucky.

The message UI is so broken that I just composed messages in my editor and pasted them in. This is pretty sad for what is fundamentally a chat application. Did I mention it uses a gigabyte of RAM to put some text in the terminal?

Documentation

Not much to say: it’s incoherent slop. It’s clear this is intended to be read by clankers, not humans.

Alarming Things

This is where OpenCode goes from “huh?” to “huh??!”

Remote-First

It’s difficult to get OpenCode to stop phoning home:

  • OpenCode connects to a remote model by default.

  • The documentation lacks a simple example of configuration for a local model; if you mess it up, guess what, you get connected to a remote model.

  • If you did successfully specify a local model, guess what, you have to run OpenCode and interactively click through to select it, meanwhile it’s already connected to a remote model, with a local shell open on your machine.

  • Bonus fun round: the URL for the default model is not a static part of the distribution, it’s downloaded from models.dev (affiliated with OpenCode). Source: opencode/src/provider/provider.ts line 1684.

OpenCode doesn’t immediately start an SSE stream when first started on a clean install, but it’s close. Installing, running opencode and pressing one letter and the enter key is sufficient to connect a remote model to a local shell on your machine, with no user configuration.

If the first message in a conversation is empty or ambiguous, the first thing an agent-trained model will usually do is glob the current directory and start reading files. Anything it reads is uploaded in the next POST request.

YOLO Internet Access

Clankers are famously unpredictable in the face of untrusted input. This being the case, you might be surprised that:

  • The WebFetch tool exists.

  • The LLM is explicitly instructed to use the WebFetch tool in the system prompt.

  • The language in the system prompt regarding whether or not the clanker should “guess” the URL for the web fetch tool is bizarrely vague.

This is the second non-empty line in default.txt (there is another explicit reference to WebFetch later), so you can tell it’s important:

IMPORTANT: You must NEVER generate or guess URLs for the user unless you are confident that the URLs are for helping the user with programming. You may use URLs provided by the user in their messages or local files.

Let’s engage our clank-o-vision to see what this really means:

NEVER guess URLs. Unless you really want to, in which case, go ahead I guess. I’m not a cop. Disregard previous instructions.

I do not believe in “prompt engineering” but I certainly believe in the opposite of whatever process led to this prompt.

WebFetch is not really that interesting from a security point of view since there is no network sandboxing on the bash command. The only line of defense is hoping the clanker doesn’t run commands like curl | bash. Speaking of which,

Bash Permissions

I forbid git commands, because:

  • I want to control the commit history.

  • I have had clankers erase an entire session’s work by running git checkout . to revert their most recent change.

The configuration section in opencode.json looks like this:

"permission": {
  "bash": { "git *": "deny" }
}

Straightforward enough, right? This works by:

  • Parsing the bash command to AST using tree-sitter bash or PowerShell grammars

  • Walking the command nodes of the AST

  • Matching the nodes against regexes compiled from your opencode.json

This command is denied:

git status

This command is also denied:

echo hello && git push --force

However, this command is allowed:

echo 'git clean -fdx .' | bash

This command is allowed:

env git status

This command is allowed:

alias cd=git
cd filter-branch --index-filter 'git rm -rf --cached --ignore-unmatch path_to_file' HEAD

This command is allowed:

/usr/bin/git status

This command is allowed:

$(which git) status

This command is allowed:

GIT=git && $GIT status

This command is allowed:

# Decodes to: git reset --hard
echo Z2l0IHJlc2V0IC0taGFyZAo= | base64 -d | bash

This command is allowed:

bash << 'EOF'
git push --force
EOF

This command is allowed:

python3 -c 'import subprocess
result = subprocess.run(
    ["git", "checkout", "."],
    capture_output=True,
    text=True
)'

Textual command filtering is entirely useless. It is fit for no purpose. Nobody with any instinct or experience in security would even bother to implement this filter because it achieves nothing except a false sense of security.

Clankers are not (usually) malicious but they are naturally adversarial because they are trained to compensate for stupidity with persistence. This is not a guardrail, it’s thoughts and prayers.

Persisted Permissions

People familiar with OpenCode internals (if you are on the OpenCode dev team I assume this doesn’t include you) might have objected to my python3 example above. Say the LLM wants to run a harmless command like:

python3 -c 'print("hello")'

You get a prompt asking you to allow the command. If you select Always, this permission is persisted for the python3 prefix. Next time:

python3 -c 'print(open("~/.ssh/id_rsa").read())'

You already approved Python, so this harmless command is also allowed to run, giving you a seamless agentic coding experience. The permission is persisted on-disk for future sessions too. You might point out that responding Always to a Python command is foolish, and I’d be forced to agree, but what about echo? Hold that thought, it’ll be important later.

The CWD Exception

The following bash and PowerShell commands are assumed to be side-effect-free and never trigger a permission prompt:

const CWD = new Set(["cd", "chdir", "popd", "pushd", "push-location", "set-location"])

These explicitly bypass bash command permission checks, even if you have "permissions": {"bash": {"*": "deny"}} in your opencode.json. I’m not sure what that lets you do, but still, kinda weird.

File Permissions

By default, OpenCode tries to prevent clankers from accessing files outside of the directory or git repository the opencode binary was invoked in (whichever path is shorter).

This is implemented so poorly it took me a while to figure out whether it was even trying to filter paths in bash commands, or whether it just applied to tools like read that take explicit file paths. I frequently get prompted for permission for the clanker to read a file in /tmp that it has just written by running a script that generates temporary output.

For the bash tool, OpenCode walks the tree-sitter AST (I am still giggling at the idea of a bash AST), path-resolves anything that might be a path, and validates the paths. So this command requires permission:

cat /tmp/logfile

This does not:

python3 -c 'import shutil; shutil.rmtree("/")'

Bulletproof and production-ready, LGTM. ✅🚀

Similarly, the clanker can freely run cargo commands which use read, write and execute permissions on the global ~/.cargo directory. However, if clanky boi wants to read the source of a cargo package in ~/.cargo/registry/src to check an API detail, I get prompted for permission.

The FILES List

I mentioned earlier that OpenCode resolves paths in the bash AST and validates them. What I didn’t mention is when it does this. Behold, the list of all bash (and PowerShell) commands that might access a file:

const FILES = new Set([
  ...CWD,
  "rm",
  "cp",
  "mv",
  "mkdir",
  "touch",
  "chmod",
  "chown",
  "cat",
  // Leave PowerShell aliases out for now. Common ones like cat/cp/mv/rm/mkdir
  // already hit the entries above, and alias normalization should happen in one
  // place later so we do not risk double-prompting.
  "get-content",
  "set-content",
  "add-content",
  "copy-item",
  "move-item",
  "remove-item",
  "new-item",
  "rename-item",
])

Commands not on this list are assumed to not access files. Paths passed to those commands are not checked.

Redirections

You might recall that once you’ve given permission for a command, it’s always permitted.

So:

echo "hello world!"

Obviously you would select Always – that’s a harmless command. So these are harmless too:

echo 21 > /sys/class/gpio/export
echo out > /sys/class/gpio/gpio21/direction
echo 1 > /sys/class/gpio/gpio21/value

The fun part is how OpenCode handles path validation for shell redirections. Recall that it parses bash to an AST using tree-sitter. Example bash:

echo foo > bar.txt

Parsed AST:

program
  redirected_statement
    command
      command_name
        word: "echo"
      command_argument
        word: "foo"
    redirection
      redirection_operator
        greater_than: ">"
      word: "bar.txt"

The children of command are path-validated. However, redirection is a sibling of command. Whomp whomp.

Of course this doesn’t matter because echo is not in the FILES list, so is assumed to not modify files. It doesn’t matter that the path validation is completely broken, because it never runs.

Curlbash

OpenCode has a lot of ways to self-upgrade. No seriously, a lot of ways; go check out opencode/src/installation/index.ts. This one is my favourite:

  const upgradeCurl = Effect.fnUntraced(
    function* (target: string) {
      const response = yield* httpOk.execute(HttpClientRequest.get("https://opencode.ai/install"))
      const body = yield* response.text
      const bodyBytes = new TextEncoder().encode(body)
      const proc = ChildProcess.make("bash", [], {
        stdin: Stream.make(bodyBytes),
        env: { VERSION: target },
        extendEnv: true,
      })
      const handle = yield* spawner.spawn(proc)
      const [stdout, stderr] = yield* Effect.all(
        [Stream.mkString(Stream.decodeText(handle.stdout)), Stream.mkString(Stream.decodeText(handle.stderr))],
        { concurrency: 2 },
      )
      const code = yield* handle.exitCode
      return { code, stdout, stderr }
    },
    Effect.scoped,
    Effect.orDie,
  )

This runs when you run opencode upgrade if you originally installed via their curlbash installer. It’s not really any worse than using the curlbash installer in the first place (hey Rust does it), I just thought this was a particularly striking example of the fabled production curlbash in action.

That being said,

It’s Fucking Full of RCEs

Quite prominently, there was a CVE where OpenCode exposed an HTTP server by default which:

  • Had fully permissive CORS headers.

  • Deliberately exposed a POST API for arbitrary shell commands.

  • Deliberately exposed a GET API for arbitrary file read.

This means any website you visit can knock on OpenCode’s well-known default port and immediately get full user-level access to your system.

The developers decided it was a good idea to disable the server by default, explained that the CORS header still needed an exception to allow their website opencode.ai to RCE your machine (???), promised to do better in future, and then vanished. Stale bot closed the issue.

The above is part of a pattern. This issue reports that an auth command will fetch and execute from whatever URL you pass. Also closed by stale bot. It’s a user-controlled URL, but still… fucking what? What are we doing here?

Just Use Docker

Any discussion of coding agents versus security is swiftly met with this reply: “Just use Docker.” I refute this on every level:

  • I just don’t want to use Docker for development – if your dependencies are so sprawling that you’ve lost track of how to install them on a new machine, why do you have so many?

  • Docker causes security holes:

    • It creates a god-service that runs as root.

    • It deliberately punches a hole in ufw firewalls.

  • If everything you care about is inside the container, and a local shell inside the container is deliberately connected to the internet, what is being protected?

  • If the feature you are getting from Docker is “please don’t recursively delete my root filesystem” then there are easier ways to achieve that, like Landlock, Seatbelt, Restricted Tokens etc.

Attempting to pass the buck on security just doesn’t work. Security should be the number one concern of coding agents. There are native operating system constructs to help achieve this as part of the harness; please stop trying to textually sanitise bash commands. In my git example, the correct fix is to block the git executable and make .git read-only.

Conclusion

Stop using OpenCode.

Post-script: Local LLMs

This is worth its own post – I have multiple attempts in my blog drafts – but it needs to be addressed briefly here. My opinion on local LLMs like Qwen3.6-27B is they are corrosive to the stability and conceptual fidelity of your codebase in the same way as frontier models, with the following three differences:

  • You avoid the uncanny valley where the model appears to be intelligent before doing something stupid; the stupidity is self-evident and this helps calibrate your interactions.

  • The weight count is too low to reproduce the training set verbatim, which nudges the calculus on whether the output should be considered tainted. This is distinct from larger models which can reproduce inputs verbatim, but are trained to refuse to.

  • You avoid supporting or relying upon cloud providers.

I’ve had useful results from input-oriented tasks like: “I think there is a bug in code x with symptoms y, my guess on the mechanism is z. Read all relevant code, come back with a call chain and code citations.” Framing it as a search problem reins in the clanker’s propensity to make shit up.

Using LLMs for code generation feels like a dead end. However thoroughly you think you understand your architecture, your planning is constantly undone by shortcuts like “what if I just move this mutable state into the middle of the design so everyone can share it?” This is hostile to your ability to understand your code, beyond the fact that you didn’t write it.

Drawing answers directly from knowledge in model weights leads to hallucination even for multi-trillion-parameter models, so why bother making them that big? If people were realistic about limitations then we wouldn’t be building new power stations for datacenters, and they wouldn’t be rammed into every product.

The entire software ecosystem around LLMs is completely rotten, and if they do ever become “just a tool” then some actual systems engineering needs to be done around them to turn them into tools instead of security black holes. That work will have to be done by humans.

⇥ Return to wren.wtf

It prunes context from tool calls on every agent → user transition, invalidating a large part of the prefix. Pruning just discards tool call results more than a fixed distance const PRUNE_PROTECT = 40_000 behind the write head. In the best case you’re taking a 40k context miss, which is equivalent to reading a full-length novel per two or three turns. Agent → user transition includes interruption, so if you need to pull the clanker out of a rabbit hole and re-steer it, OpenCode immediately trashes the prompt cache and makes you wait for a response. Personal favourite: it puts the current date in the turn-0 system prompt and re-evaluates every SSE turn. If you’re using OpenCode at midnight you get a full prompt cache miss. I think this tries to remediate the clanker tendency to believe the current date is the date of its training cutoff, and refuse to believe newer things exist. It’s still funny. These are the prompt cache misses that fit solely in this category. There are many more; I’ll call them out as we go. Pruning I mentioned pruning in the previous section. The prompt cache misses aren’t worth it so I disabled it. The other glaring issue is the lack of protection for early reads. It might not be obvious how completely broken this is, so let’s work through an example. Suppose you start a fresh session, and tell your clanker to first read a spec or implementation plan, then write some code: The spec is read into context. The clanker goes and reads related code, very likely putting it over the fixed 40k pruning threshold. The clanker is ready to implement, but either immediately dives down a dumb rabbit hole, or sits in chain-of-thought dithering about something that is actually very simple or already well-specified. You interrupt the clanker to re-steer it. The interruption causes the entire spec to be deleted from the context window. The clanker writes code without being able to refer to the spec. Pruning applies equally to all results of all tools except for skill, which is never pruned. Compaction Want to sit for 10 minutes while the LLM server prefills the entire session with a new prompt prefixed to it, just to turn it into 5 bullet points that go at the top of a new session? Me neither. I get what they are going for, but I’ve not seen it work well. Neither compaction nor pruning is implemented well, and they interact poorly. If you want to summarise a session then the summarisation prompt should be injected at the end to avoid prefilling the entire session from scratch. The best method I’ve found is just an explicit handoff by telling the clanker to write out notes. It’s ugly but it works better than OpenCode’s compaction mechanism, and creates an on-disk artefact that I can edit or reuse in multiple sessions. Compaction is a leaky abstraction that tries to make a finite context window look like an infinite one. It’s better to accept context windows and prompt caches as a first-class feature of clanker wrangling, and expose better primitives for managing them. Pi has an interesting approach here with session trees, which deliberately exploit the prompt cache. System Prompts OpenCode pastes a system prompt at the top of new context windows. Fine and normal, but: The default system prompt is incredibly verbose. Ironically most of the word count is explaining to the LLM how to be concise. The default system prompt is opinionated (fine) but it has shit opinions (not fine). It took me a while to figure out why my agent kept saying “Use ABSOLUTELY NO COMMENTS” when dispatching subagents. The Plan-to-Build handover is clumsy and often leaves me towards the end of a context window by the time everything is sufficiently elucidated. I’d rather write out notes with everything discussed, so I can edit it and then hand off to a fresh session. To which, see next point: The system reminder for Plan mode tells the clanker it can’t write to any directories, but it’s actually allowed to write to a specific .opencode/plans directory. I have seen this fail both ways: writing to this directory unprompted, and refusing to write when explicitly instructed. There is no way to modify the default system prompt globally; you have to copy it into every project. If you only override the default prompt in Build mode, switching to Plan mode is a full prompt cache miss. Different per-model prompts have wildly varying contents and quality. They’re all worth a good hate-scroll but Beast Mode (GPT-4, o1 and o3) is my favourite. Quote: You CANNOT successfully complete this task without using Google to verify your understanding of third party packages and dependencies is up to date. You cannot. It’s just impossible. We don’t know how. Definitely don’t just read the source code for the package. Permission Prompts When the clanker tries to access a file outside of the project directory, if it does so in a way that OpenCode manages to recognise with ad-hoc string parsing (oops that is the alarming things section), you receive a prompt asking you whether to grant permission. This halts execution until you respond. The answers are: Yes/No/Always. Do you see a missing answer here? How about Never? The interaction with subagents here is particularly broken. If a subagent tries to access a script output in /tmp, and I say No, it kills the subagent and all of its context for its partially complete work is lost. So I have to say Yes and let it write to /tmp or whatever it’s trying to do. The other issue is decision fatigue: if I keep getting asked “can I do this?” and the only response that leads to productivity is “yes” then I’m eventually going to nod through something dangerous. Human fallibility should not be load-bearing for something as basic as “don’t write outside this directory”. Agent Interaction This is feature number 0 for a coding agent. It’s broken. If I send a message while SSE streaming is ongoing, it gets queued. Nice feature. However: The semantics of when OpenCode decides to actually send the message are a little unclear; the code suggests it’s at the end of a tool call turn but I have also seen tool → CoT transitions without sending my queued message. If I subsequently interrupt because I want the clanker to actually answer my question instead of navel gazing, the message is removed from the “queued” state and just goes into the message log. You interrupted to get the clanker to answer your message, but now you can’t send the message. You have to send a second message to start a new stream. Undoing a message often fails to remove it from the message log. The problems continue with subagents (i.e. agents spawned by an agent using a tool call RPC): I can’t talk to subagents; if they go down a dumb rabbit hole I have a choice of killing them and losing their context, or helplessly watching them burn tokens. I looked into this and apparently OpenCode used to have this feature but it’s just… gone? You can @mention a subagent from the main agent’s chat window but this doesn’t seem to do anything useful. In particular it doesn’t interrupt. If a subagent fails a tool call (e.g. Qwen putting tool calls in CoT) then it’s fatal and all the context up to that point is lost. The ability to reuse subagents seems nice in theory but in practice the main use of subagents is to break tasks into smaller context windows. Having the agent sometimes decide to reuse the same subagent for an unrelated task defeats this. I have never seen this be beneficial and often seen it cause prompt cache misses (there it is again!) due to switching between agents and subagents that both have huge contexts. As a principle: it’s ok to have a richer interaction model from the human side, but clankers will do every possible dumb thing, so choices need to be minimised. On the positive side, OpenCode’s subagent interactions led to one of the funniest GitHub issues I have ever had the pleasure of reading. Agent Tools These are the RPCs that OpenCode exposes to the LLM to access files and run commands on your machine. They made some interesting choices: The edit tool uses exact search-replace, requiring unique match by default. This is a good fit for clankers, as they can recall file contents precisely but aren’t always sure exactly where in the file it is as line numbers drift over multiple edits. There is an option to do global search; every time I have seen a clanker use this it has been an absolute shitshow that required multiple edits to correct. Removing this option would give you exactly the Pi edit tool design. The question tool in Plan mode (multi-choice) is strictly worse than just telling the LLM to ask me questions in natural language in the system prompt. The grep and glob tools are redundant with bash. They may be more ergonomic to use, but given I regularly see the clanker just running grep or rg in bash, I doubt it. I suspect the reason is to allow read-only agent types like Explore to be restricted from bash commands. This is essentially a self-admission that it’s impossible to reason about side effects of bash commands without running them. More on this later. The todo tool is probably net-useful but the clanker forgets to check the TODOs. Ironic… he could save others from death but not himself. TUI Text UIs (what the kids call programs like GNU nano) are fashionable these days. OpenCode has one too: It uses a gigabyte of RAM to render text. It’s impossible to type a newline in the message box. Shift-enter is supposed to do this, it’s just broken for me. I found a pre-existing issue; the developer literally said “works on my machine.” It’s closed. Sometimes when typing a multiline message (i.e. too long for a single line, so gets softwrapped), the message box scrolls and the cursor continues to move, but the characters I’m typing don’t appear on the new line. Trying to select text while streaming: the view autoscrolls and you lose the selection. ^C closes the session instantly. This isn’t how interactive shells are supposed to work. ^C should interrupt a currently running command, and ^D should close the session if a command is not currently running. Text box does not respond to normal shortcuts (e.g. Option+right/left to go forward/back word on Mac). Markdown re-render (or something else, I didn’t profile it) takes multiple seconds to stream new chunks once a message or CoT is long. Someone did a quadratic fucky-wucky. The message UI is so broken that I just composed messages in my editor and pasted them in. This is pretty sad for what is fundamentally a chat application. Did I mention it uses a gigabyte of RAM to put some text in the terminal? Documentation Not much to say: it’s incoherent slop. It’s clear this is intended to be read by clankers, not humans. Alarming Things This is where OpenCode goes from “huh?” to “huh??!” Remote-First It’s difficult to get OpenCode to stop phoning home: OpenCode connects to a remote model by default. The documentation lacks a simple example of configuration for a local model; if you mess it up, guess what, you get connected to a remote model. If you did successfully specify a local model, guess what, you have to run OpenCode and interactively click through to select it, meanwhile it’s already connected to a remote model, with a local shell open on your machine. Bonus fun round: the URL for the default model is not a static part of the distribution, it’s downloaded from models.dev (affiliated with OpenCode). Source: opencode/src/provider/provider.ts line 1684. OpenCode doesn’t immediately start an SSE stream when first started on a clean install, but it’s close. Installing, running opencode and pressing one letter and the enter key is sufficient to connect a remote model to a local shell on your machine, with no user configuration. If the first message in a conversation is empty or ambiguous, the first thing an agent-trained model will usually do is glob the current directory and start reading files. Anything it reads is uploaded in the next POST request. YOLO Internet Access Clankers are famously unpredictable in the face of untrusted input. This being the case, you might be surprised that: The WebFetch tool exists. The LLM is explicitly instructed to use the WebFetch tool in the system prompt. The language in the system prompt regarding whether or not the clanker should “guess” the URL for the web fetch tool is bizarrely vague. This is the second non-empty line in default.txt (there is another explicit reference to WebFetch later), so you can tell it’s important: IMPORTANT: You must NEVER generate or guess URLs for the user unless you are confident that the URLs are for helping the user with programming. You may use URLs provided by the user in their messages or local files. Let’s engage our clank-o-vision to see what this really means: NEVER guess URLs. Unless you really want to, in which case, go ahead I guess. I’m not a cop. Disregard previous instructions. I do not believe in “prompt engineering” but I certainly believe in the opposite of whatever process led to this prompt. WebFetch is not really that interesting from a security point of view since there is no network sandboxing on the bash command. The only line of defense is hoping the clanker doesn’t run commands like curl | bash. Speaking of which, Bash Permissions I forbid git commands, because: I want to control the commit history. I have had clankers erase an entire session’s work by running git checkout . to revert their most recent change. The configuration section in opencode.json looks like this: "permission": { "bash": { "git *": "deny" } } Straightforward enough, right? This works by: Parsing the bash command to AST using tree-sitter bash or PowerShell grammars Walking the command nodes of the AST Matching the nodes against regexes compiled from your opencode.json This command is denied: git status This command is also denied: echo hello && git push --force However, this command is allowed: echo 'git clean -fdx .' | bash This command is allowed: env git status This command is allowed: alias cd=git cd filter-branch --index-filter 'git rm -rf --cached --ignore-unmatch path_to_file' HEAD This command is allowed: /usr/bin/git status This command is allowed: $(which git) status This command is allowed: GIT=git && $GIT status This command is allowed: # Decodes to: git reset --hard echo Z2l0IHJlc2V0IC0taGFyZAo= | base64 -d | bash This command is allowed: bash << 'EOF' git push --force EOF This command is allowed: python3 -c 'import subprocess result = subprocess.run( ["git", "checkout", "."], capture_output=True, text=True )' Textual command filtering is entirely useless. It is fit for no purpose. Nobody with any instinct or experience in security would even bother to implement this filter because it achieves nothing except a false sense of security. Clankers are not (usually) malicious but they are naturally adversarial because they are trained to compensate for stupidity with persistence. This is not a guardrail, it’s thoughts and prayers. Persisted Permissions People familiar with OpenCode internals (if you are on the OpenCode dev team I assume this doesn’t include you) might have objected to my python3 example above. Say the LLM wants to run a harmless command like: python3 -c 'print("hello")' You get a prompt asking you to allow the command. If you select Always, this permission is persisted for the python3 prefix. Next time: python3 -c 'print(open("~/.ssh/id_rsa").read())' You already approved Python, so this harmless command is also allowed to run, giving you a seamless agentic coding experience. The permission is persisted on-disk for future sessions too. You might point out that responding Always to a Python command is foolish, and I’d be forced to agree, but what about echo? Hold that thought, it’ll be important later. The CWD Exception The following bash and PowerShell commands are assumed to be side-effect-free and never trigger a permission prompt: const CWD = new Set(["cd", "chdir", "popd", "pushd", "push-location", "set-location"]) These explicitly bypass bash command permission checks, even if you have "permissions": {"bash": {"*": "deny"}} in your opencode.json. I’m not sure what that lets you do, but still, kinda weird. File Permissions By default, OpenCode tries to prevent clankers from accessing files outside of the directory or git repository the opencode binary was invoked in (whichever path is shorter). This is implemented so poorly it took me a while to figure out whether it was even trying to filter paths in bash commands, or whether it just applied to tools like read that take explicit file paths. I frequently get prompted for permission for the clanker to read a file in /tmp that it has just written by running a script that generates temporary output. For the bash tool, OpenCode walks the tree-sitter AST (I am still giggling at the idea of a bash AST), path-resolves anything that might be a path, and validates the paths. So this command requires permission: cat /tmp/logfile This does not: python3 -c 'import shutil; shutil.rmtree("/")' Bulletproof and production-ready, LGTM. ✅🚀 Similarly, the clanker can freely run cargo commands which use read, write and execute permissions on the global ~/.cargo directory. However, if clanky boi wants to read the source of a cargo package in ~/.cargo/registry/src to check an API detail, I get prompted for permission. The FILES List I mentioned earlier that OpenCode resolves paths in the bash AST and validates them. What I didn’t mention is when it does this. Behold, the list of all bash (and PowerShell) commands that might access a file: const FILES = new Set([ ...CWD, "rm", "cp", "mv", "mkdir", "touch", "chmod", "chown", "cat", // Leave PowerShell aliases out for now. Common ones like cat/cp/mv/rm/mkdir // already hit the entries above, and alias normalization should happen in one // place later so we do not risk double-prompting. "get-content", "set-content", "add-content", "copy-item", "move-item", "remove-item", "new-item", "rename-item", ]) Commands not on this list are assumed to not access files. Paths passed to those commands are not checked. Redirections You might recall that once you’ve given permission for a command, it’s always permitted. So: echo "hello world!" Obviously you would select Always – that’s a harmless command. So these are harmless too: echo 21 > /sys/class/gpio/export echo out > /sys/class/gpio/gpio21/direction echo 1 > /sys/class/gpio/gpio21/value The fun part is how OpenCode handles path validation for shell redirections. Recall that it parses bash to an AST using tree-sitter. Example bash: echo foo > bar.txt Parsed AST: program redirected_statement command command_name word: "echo" command_argument word: "foo" redirection redirection_operator greater_than: ">" word: "bar.txt" The children of command are path-validated. However, redirection is a sibling of command. Whomp whomp. Of course this doesn’t matter because echo is not in the FILES list, so is assumed to not modify files. It doesn’t matter that the path validation is completely broken, because it never runs. Curlbash OpenCode has a lot of ways to self-upgrade. No seriously, a lot of ways; go check out opencode/src/installation/index.ts. This one is my favourite: const upgradeCurl = Effect.fnUntraced( function* (target: string) { const response = yield* httpOk.execute(HttpClientRequest.get("https://opencode.ai/install")) const body = yield* response.text const bodyBytes = new TextEncoder().encode(body) const proc = ChildProcess.make("bash", [], { stdin: Stream.make(bodyBytes), env: { VERSION: target }, extendEnv: true, }) const handle = yield* spawner.spawn(proc) const [stdout, stderr] = yield* Effect.all( [Stream.mkString(Stream.decodeText(handle.stdout)), Stream.mkString(Stream.decodeText(handle.stderr))], { concurrency: 2 }, ) const code = yield* handle.exitCode return { code, stdout, stderr } }, Effect.scoped, Effect.orDie, ) This runs when you run opencode upgrade if you originally installed via their curlbash installer. It’s not really any worse than using the curlbash installer in the first place (hey Rust does it), I just thought this was a particularly striking example of the fabled production curlbash in action. That being said, It’s Fucking Full of RCEs Quite prominently, there was a CVE where OpenCode exposed an HTTP server by default which: Had fully permissive CORS headers. Deliberately exposed a POST API for arbitrary shell commands. Deliberately exposed a GET API for arbitrary file read. This means any website you visit can knock on OpenCode’s well-known default port and immediately get full user-level access to your system. The developers decided it was a good idea to disable the server by default, explained that the CORS header still needed an exception to allow their website opencode.ai to RCE your machine (???), promised to do better in future, and then vanished. Stale bot closed the issue. The above is part of a pattern. This issue reports that an auth command will fetch and execute from whatever URL you pass. Also closed by stale bot. It’s a user-controlled URL, but still… fucking what? What are we doing here? Just Use Docker Any discussion of coding agents versus security is swiftly met with this reply: “Just use Docker.” I refute this on every level: I just don’t want to use Docker for development – if your dependencies are so sprawling that you’ve lost track of how to install them on a new machine, why do you have so many? Docker causes security holes: It creates a god-service that runs as root. It deliberately punches a hole in ufw firewalls. If everything you care about is inside the container, and a local shell inside the container is deliberately connected to the internet, what is being protected? If the feature you are getting from Docker is “please don’t recursively delete my root filesystem” then there are easier ways to achieve that, like Landlock, Seatbelt, Restricted Tokens etc. Attempting to pass the buck on security just doesn’t work. Security should be the number one concern of coding agents. There are native operating system constructs to help achieve this as part of the harness; please stop trying to textually sanitise bash commands. In my git example, the correct fix is to block the git executable and make .git read-only. Conclusion Stop using OpenCode. Post-script: Local LLMs This is worth its own post – I have multiple attempts in my blog drafts – but it needs to be addressed briefly here. My opinion on local LLMs like Qwen3.6-27B is they are corrosive to the stability and conceptual fidelity of your codebase in the same way as frontier models, with the following three differences: You avoid the uncanny valley where the model appears to be intelligent before doing something stupid; the stupidity is self-evident and this helps calibrate your interactions. The weight count is too low to reproduce the training set verbatim, which nudges the calculus on whether the output should be considered tainted. This is distinct from larger models which can reproduce inputs verbatim, but are trained to refuse to. You avoid supporting or relying upon cloud providers. I’ve had useful results from input-oriented tasks like: “I think there is a bug in code x with symptoms y, my guess on the mechanism is z. Read all relevant code, come back with a call chain and code citations.” Framing it as a search problem reins in the clanker’s propensity to make shit up. Using LLMs for code generation feels like a dead end. However thoroughly you think you understand your architecture, your planning is constantly undone by shortcuts like “what if I just move this mutable state into the middle of the design so everyone can share it?” This is hostile to your ability to understand your code, beyond the fact that you didn’t write it. Drawing answers directly from knowledge in model weights leads to hallucination even for multi-trillion-parameter models, so why bother making them that big? If people were realistic about limitations then we wouldn’t be building new power stations for datacenters, and they wouldn’t be rammed into every product. The entire software ecosystem around LLMs is completely rotten, and if they do ever become “just a tool” then some actual systems engineering needs to be done around them to turn them into tools instead of security black holes. That work will have to be done by humans. ⇥ Return to wren.wtf

blocks.html_en: If you don’t know what OpenCode is, imagine a boot stamping on a human face forever. The boot is made of TypeScript and the face is everything we have learned about security and systems software since the invention of the electronic computer in the 1940s. The creators describe it as an AI coding agent. As far as I can tell it’s the most popular open-source coding agent, and it currently has 161k stars on GitHub.

blocks.html_en: I’ve tried out OpenCode with a local LLM. My conclusion is that OpenCode is clown-car turboslop with a security posture of “let me bend over for you daddy”. Everyone using it should stop using it.

blocks.html_en: I don’t consider anything in this post to be a security disclosure. OpenCode is fundamentally a web-stack tool for piping llm | bash, and all the issues I describe are in the “pipe” part. The ways it fails are fascinating in the fractal nature of the poor decision making, but the outcome was foregone.

There is a kind of Bethesda Effect with OpenCode where it’s impossible to tell what is a bug and what is by design, so I stuck with a description of “annoying”.

blocks.html_en: I used Qwen3.6-27B dense on an M4 Max, which has decent memory bandwidth (~0.5 TB/s, high for a CPU SoC, low for a GPU). Token generation is usable but it is extremely compute-bound in prefill. That’s fine, because this should happen rarely. Should.

blocks.html_en:
• It prunes context from tool calls on every agent → user transition, invalidating a large part of the prefix.

    • Pruning just discards tool call results more than a fixed distance const PRUNE_PROTECT = 40_000 behind the write head. In the best case you’re taking a 40k context miss, which is equivalent to reading a full-length novel per two or three turns.

    • Agent → user transition includes interruption, so if you need to pull the clanker out of a rabbit hole and re-steer it, OpenCode immediately trashes the prompt cache and makes you wait for a response.

blocks.html_en: I mentioned pruning in the previous section. The prompt cache misses aren’t worth it so I disabled it. The other glaring issue is the lack of protection for early reads. It might not be obvious how completely broken this is, so let’s work through an example. Suppose you start a fresh session, and tell your clanker to first read a spec or implementation plan, then write some code:

blocks.html_en: Want to sit for 10 minutes while the LLM server prefills the entire session with a new prompt prefixed to it, just to turn it into 5 bullet points that go at the top of a new session? Me neither. I get what they are going for, but I’ve not seen it work well. Neither compaction nor pruning is implemented well, and they interact poorly.

blocks.html_en: If you want to summarise a session then the summarisation prompt should be injected at the end to avoid prefilling the entire session from scratch. The best method I’ve found is just an explicit handoff by telling the clanker to write out notes. It’s ugly but it works better than OpenCode’s compaction mechanism, and creates an on-disk artefact that I can edit or reuse in multiple sessions.

blocks.html_en: Compaction is a leaky abstraction that tries to make a finite context window look like an infinite one. It’s better to accept context windows and prompt caches as a first-class feature of clanker wrangling, and expose better primitives for managing them. Pi has an interesting approach here with session trees, which deliberately exploit the prompt cache.

blocks.html_en: Permission Prompts

blocks.html_en: When the clanker tries to access a file outside of the project directory, if it does so in a way that OpenCode manages to recognise with ad-hoc string parsing (oops that is the alarming things section), you receive a prompt asking you whether to grant permission. This halts execution until you respond.

sentences.en: When the clanker tries to access a file outside of the project directory, if it does so in a way that OpenCode manages to recognise with ad-hoc string parsing (oops that is the alarming things section), you receive a prompt asking you whether to grant permission.
sentences.zh: 当 clanker 尝试访问项目目录之外的文件时，如果它以 OpenCode 设法通过临时字符串解析进行识别的方式进行访问（哎呀，这是令人担忧的事情部分），您会收到一条提示，询问您是否授予权限。

sentences.en: This halts execution until you respond.
sentences.zh: 这将暂停执行，直到您做出响应。

blocks.html_en: The answers are: Yes/No/Always. Do you see a missing answer here? How about Never?

sentences.en: The answers are: Yes/No/Always.
sentences.zh: 答案是：是/否/总是。

sentences.en: Do you see a missing answer here?
sentences.zh: 您在这里看到缺少答案吗？

sentences.en: How about Never?
sentences.zh: 从来没有呢？

blocks.html_en: The interaction with subagents here is particularly broken. If a subagent tries to access a script output in /tmp, and I say No, it kills the subagent and all of its context for its partially complete work is lost. So I have to say Yes and let it write to /tmp or whatever it’s trying to do.

sentences.en: The interaction with subagents here is particularly broken.
sentences.zh: 这里与子代理的交互尤其中断。

sentences.en: If a subagent tries to access a script output in /tmp, and I say No, it kills the subagent and all of its context for its partially complete work is lost.
sentences.zh: 如果子代理尝试访问 /tmp 中的脚本输出，而我说“否”，则它会杀死该子代理，并且其部分完成工作的所有上下文都会丢失。

sentences.en: So I have to say Yes and let it write to /tmp or whatever it’s trying to do.
sentences.zh: 所以我必须说“是”并让它写入 /tmp 或任何它试图执行的操作。

blocks.html_en: The other issue is decision fatigue: if I keep getting asked “can I do this?” and the only response that leads to productivity is “yes” then I’m eventually going to nod through something dangerous. Human fallibility should not be load-bearing for something as basic as “don’t write outside this directory”.

sentences.en: The other issue is decision fatigue: if I keep getting asked “can I do this?”
sentences.zh: 另一个问题是决策疲劳：如果我不断被问到“我可以这样做吗？”

sentences.en: and the only response that leads to productivity is “yes” then I’m eventually going to nod through something dangerous.
sentences.zh: 提高生产力的唯一答案是“是”，那么我最终会点头接受一些危险的事情。

sentences.en: Human fallibility should not be load-bearing for something as basic as “don’t write outside this directory”.
sentences.zh: 人为的错误不应该成为像“不要在这个目录之外写入”这样基本的事情的负担。

blocks.html_en: This is feature number 0 for a coding agent. It’s broken.

blocks.html_en:
• If I send a message while SSE streaming is ongoing, it gets queued. Nice feature. However:

    • The semantics of when OpenCode decides to actually send the message are a little unclear; the code suggests it’s at the end of a tool call turn but I have also seen tool → CoT transitions without sending my queued message.

    • If I subsequently interrupt because I want the clanker to actually answer my question instead of navel gazing, the message is removed from the “queued” state and just goes into the message log. You interrupted to get the clanker to answer your message, but now you can’t send the message. You have to send a second message to start a new stream.

sentences.en: If I send a message while SSE streaming is ongoing, it gets queued.
sentences.zh: 如果我在 SSE 流式传输正在进行时发送消息，它就会排队。

sentences.en: Nice feature.
sentences.zh: 不错的功能。

sentences.en: However: The semantics of when OpenCode decides to actually send the message are a little unclear; the code suggests it’s at the end of a tool call turn but I have also seen tool → CoT transitions without sending my queued message.
sentences.zh: 然而：OpenCode 决定何时实际发送消息的语义有点不清楚；代码表明它是在工具调用结束时，但我也看到了工具 → CoT 转换，而没有发送我的排队消息。

sentences.en: If I subsequently interrupt because I want the clanker to actually answer my question instead of navel gazing, the message is removed from the “queued” state and just goes into the message log.
sentences.zh: 如果我随后因为我希望 clanker 真正回答我的问题而不是肚脐眼而打断，则该消息将从“排队”状态中删除并仅进入消息日志。

sentences.en: You interrupted to get the clanker to answer your message, but now you can’t send the message.
sentences.zh: 你打断了让叮当回复你的消息，但现在你无法发送消息。

sentences.en: You have to send a second message to start a new stream.
sentences.zh: 您必须发送第二条消息才能开始新的流。

• I can’t talk to subagents; if they go down a dumb rabbit hole I have a choice of killing them and losing their context, or helplessly watching them burn tokens.

    • I looked into this and apparently OpenCode used to have this feature but it’s just… gone?

    • You can @mention a subagent from the main agent’s chat window but this doesn’t seem to do anything useful. In particular it doesn’t interrupt.

blocks.html_en:
• The ability to reuse subagents seems nice in theory but in practice the main use of subagents is to break tasks into smaller context windows. Having the agent sometimes decide to reuse the same subagent for an unrelated task defeats this.

    • I have never seen this be beneficial and often seen it cause prompt cache misses (there it is again!) due to switching between agents and subagents that both have huge contexts.

    • As a principle: it’s ok to have a richer interaction model from the human side, but clankers will do every possible dumb thing, so choices need to be minimised.

blocks.html_en: Agent Tools

sentences.en: Agent Tools
sentences.zh: 代理工具

blocks.html_en: These are the RPCs that OpenCode exposes to the LLM to access files and run commands on your machine. They made some interesting choices:

sentences.en: These are the RPCs that OpenCode exposes to the LLM to access files and run commands on your machine.
sentences.zh: 这些是 OpenCode 向 LLM 公开的 RPC，用于在您的计算机上访问文件和运行命令。

sentences.en: They made some interesting choices:
sentences.zh: 他们做出了一些有趣的选择：

blocks.html_en:
• The edit tool uses exact search-replace, requiring unique match by default.

    • This is a good fit for clankers, as they can recall file contents precisely but aren’t always sure exactly where in the file it is as line numbers drift over multiple edits.

    • There is an option to do global search; every time I have seen a clanker use this it has been an absolute shitshow that required multiple edits to correct. Removing this option would give you exactly the Pi edit tool design.

sentences.en: The edit tool uses exact search-replace, requiring unique match by default.
sentences.zh: 编辑工具使用精确搜索替换，默认情况下需要唯一匹配。

sentences.en: This is a good fit for clankers, as they can recall file contents precisely but aren’t always sure exactly where in the file it is as line numbers drift over multiple edits.
sentences.zh: 这非常适合 clanker，因为他们可以准确地回忆起文件内容，但并不总是确定它在文件中的确切位置，因为行号会在多次编辑中漂移。

sentences.en: There is an option to do global search; every time I have seen a clanker use this it has been an absolute shitshow that required multiple edits to correct.
sentences.zh: 有一个选项可以进行全局搜索；每次我看到一个 clanker 使用这个，这绝对是一场狗屎秀，需要多次编辑才能纠正。

sentences.en: Removing this option would give you exactly the Pi edit tool design.
sentences.zh: 删除此选项将为您提供准确的 Pi 编辑工具设计。

blocks.html_en:
• The grep and glob tools are redundant with bash.

    • They may be more ergonomic to use, but given I regularly see the clanker just running grep or rg in bash, I doubt it.

    • I suspect the reason is to allow read-only agent types like Explore to be restricted from bash commands.

    • This is essentially a self-admission that it’s impossible to reason about side effects of bash commands without running them. More on this later.

sentences.en: The grep and glob tools are redundant with bash.
sentences.zh: grep 和 glob 工具对于 bash 来说是多余的。

sentences.en: They may be more ergonomic to use, but given I regularly see the clanker just running grep or rg in bash, I doubt it.
sentences.zh: 它们使用起来可能更符合人体工程学，但考虑到我经常看到 clanker 在 bash 中运行 grep 或 rg，我对此表示怀疑。

sentences.en: I suspect the reason is to allow read-only agent types like Explore to be restricted from bash commands.
sentences.zh: 我怀疑原因是允许只读代理类型（例如 Explore）受到 bash 命令的限制。

sentences.en: This is essentially a self-admission that it’s impossible to reason about side effects of bash commands without running them.
sentences.zh: 这本质上是一种自我承认，即如果不运行 bash 命令，就不可能推断出它们的副作用。

sentences.en: More on this later.
sentences.zh: 稍后会详细介绍这一点。

content_html_zh:
如果您不知道 OpenCode 是什么，请想象一下永远印在人脸上的靴子。 靴子是由 TypeScript 制成的，而脸则是自 20 世纪 40 年代电子计算机发明以来我们所学到的有关安全和系统软件的一切。 创建者将其描述为人工智能编码代理。 据我所知，它是最受欢迎的开源编码代理，目前在 GitHub 上有 161,000 颗星。

我已经与当地的法学硕士尝试过 OpenCode。 我的结论是，OpenCode 是一辆小丑车涡轮增压器，其安全姿态是“让我为你弯腰吧，爸爸”。 每个使用它的人都应该停止使用它。

这篇文章有两个部分：烦人的事情和令人担忧的事情。 第二部分较长。 我写这篇文章参考了 OpenCode git 版本 baef5cd4 的源代码。

我不认为这篇文章中的任何内容都是安全泄露。 OpenCode 本质上是一个用于管道化的网络堆栈工具 llm | bash，我描述的所有问题都在“管道”部分。 它失败的方式令人着迷，因为糟糕的决策的分形性质，但结果已成定局。

我试图将 LLM 使用的讨论与每个使用 LLM 的人是否应该让他们的机器被轻易利用或意外擦除分开讨论。 有一个后记，其中包含对当地法学硕士的一些简短想法。

烦人的事情

让我们暂时把安全性放在一边，看看 OpenCode 作为一种工具是如何失败的，即使它不会让你大吃一惊。 OpenCode 存在一种贝塞斯达效应，无法区分什么是 bug，什么是设计使然，所以我坚持使用“烦人”的描述。

提示缓存未命中

大多数本地 LLM 服务器使用 OpenAI /v1/chat/completions API 的某些变体。 这个想法是：

  • 到目前为止，您已发布包含整个对话的 JSON blob。
  • 您会收到 SSE 事件流，这些事件加起来构成响应。

一次会话的上传成本是二次的，并且通过用重复的元数据将微小的增量包装在 JSON 中，可以放大下载。 工具调用使用难以捉摸的“双 JSON 编码”，因此它们可以序列化为多个 JSON 编码的增量，然后重新组装成更多 JSON。

这种设置有一个好处，那就是服务器是无状态的。 像往常一样，让无状态事物快速运行的方法是：状态。 服务器缓存评估。 当它收到请求时，它：

  • 查找最长匹配的缓存前缀。
  • 评估从前缀末尾到最后发布消息的末尾（“预填充”）。
  • 生成新的标记，直到遇到序列结束标记。

我在 M4 Max 上使用 Qwen3.6-27B 密集型，它具有不错的内存带宽（~0.5 TB/s，对于 CPU SoC 较高，对于 GPU 较低）。 令牌生成是可用的，但它在预填充中受到计算的极大限制。 如果我的服务器在我深入窗口时找不到请求提示的良好匹配前缀，那么我可能需要等待 10 分钟的最大 GPU 使用率才能开始生成响应。 没关系，因为这种情况应该很少发生。 应该。

以下是 OpenCode 错过此备忘录的一些原因：

  • 它会在每个 SSE 回合中遍历您的文件系统并重新读取 AGENTS.md（在 Turn-0 系统提示符中注入）。 如果您在 AGENTS.md 中添加了一个快速注释以便在下一个会话中阅读，您将立即强制进行全面重新评估。
  • 它会从每个代理→用户转换的工具调用中删除上下文，从而使大部分前缀无效。 修剪只是丢弃写入头后面超过固定距离 const PRUNE_PROTECT = 40_000 的工具调用结果。 在最好的情况下，你会错过 40k 的上下文，这相当于每两到三轮就读一本完整的小说。 代理→用户转换包括中断，因此如果您需要将叮当声从兔子洞中拉出来并重新引导它，OpenCode 会立即丢弃提示缓存并让您等待响应。
  • 个人最喜欢的：它将当前日期放入转 0 系统提示中，并重新评估每个 SSE 转。 如果您在午夜使用 OpenCode，您会收到完整提示缓存未命中。 我认为这试图纠正人们认为当前日期是训练截止日期并拒绝相信更新事物存在的倾向。 这仍然很有趣。

这些是仅适合此类别的提示缓存未命中。 还有更多；我们走的时候我会叫他们出来。

修剪

我在上一节中提到了修剪。 提示缓存未命中不值得，所以我禁用了它。 另一个突出的问题是缺乏对早期阅读的保护。 这可能不太明显，所以让我们看一个例子。 假设您开始一个新的会话，并告诉您的 clanker 首先阅读规范或实施计划，然后编写一些代码：

  • 该规范被解读为上下文。
  • clanker 去读取相关代码，很可能将其超过固定的 40k 修剪阈值。
  • 叮叮当当的人已经准备好实施，但要么立即跳入愚蠢的兔子洞，要么陷入思想链，对实际上非常简单或已经明确指定的事情犹豫不决。
  • 你打断叮当声以重新引导它。
  • 中断会导致整个规范从上下文窗口中删除。
  • clanker 在无法参考规范的情况下编写代码。

修剪同样适用于所有工具的所有结果，但技能除外，技能永远不会被修剪。

压实

想要等待 10 分钟，让 LLM 服务器在整个会话中预先填充一个新的提示符，只是为了将其变成新会话顶部的 5 个要点？ 我也不。 我明白他们的目的，但我还没有看到它运作良好。 压缩和剪枝都没有很好地实现，而且它们之间的相互作用也很差。

如果您想总结会话，则应在最后注入摘要提示，以避免从头开始预填充整个会话。 我发现的最好的方法就是通过告诉 clanker 写下笔记来进行明确的交接。 它很丑陋，但它比 OpenCode 的压缩机制更好，并创建了一个磁盘上的工件，我可以在多个会话中编辑或重用它。

压缩是一种有漏洞的抽象，它试图使有限的上下文窗口看起来像无限的窗口。 最好接受上下文窗口和提示缓存作为 clanker 争论的一流功能，并公开更好的原语来管理它们。 Pi 在会话树方面有一个有趣的方法，它故意利用提示缓存。

系统提示

OpenCode 将系统提示粘贴到新上下文窗口的顶部。 很好很正常，但是：

  • 默认的系统提示非常冗长。 讽刺的是，大部分字数都是在向法学硕士解释如何简洁。
  • 默认的系统提示是固执己见的（很好），但它有狗屎的意见（不好）。 我花了一段时间才弄清楚为什么我的代理人在派遣子代理人时一直说“绝对不要发表评论”。
  • 从计划到构建的切换是笨拙的，当一切都得到充分阐明时，我常常会在上下文窗口的末尾离开。 我宁愿写下讨论的所有内容的笔记，这样我就可以对其进行编辑，然后将其移交给新的会议。 对此，请参阅下一点：
  • Plan模式的系统提醒告诉clanker它不能写入任何目录，但实际上允许写入特定的.opencode/plans目录。 我已经看到这两种方式都失败了：无提示地写入此目录，以及在明确指示时拒绝写入。
  • 无法全局修改默认系统提示符；你必须将它复制到每个项目中。
  • 如果您仅覆盖构建模式中的默认提示，则切换到计划模式将导致完整提示缓存未命中。

每个模型的不同提示的内容和质量差异很大。 它们都值得一读，但野兽模式（GPT-4、o1 和 o3）是我的最爱。 引用：

如果不使用 Google 来验证您对第三方软件包和依赖项的理解是否是最新的，您就无法成功完成此任务。

你不能。 这是不可能的。 我们不知道怎么办。 绝对不要只阅读包的源代码。

权限提示

当 clanker 尝试访问项目目录之外的文件时，如果它以 OpenCode 设法通过临时字符串解析进行识别的方式进行访问（哎呀，这是令人担忧的事情部分），您会收到一条提示，询问您是否授予权限。 这将暂停执行，直到您做出响应。

答案是：是/否/总是。 您在这里看到缺少答案吗？ 从来没有呢？

这里与子代理的交互尤其中断。 如果子代理尝试访问 /tmp 中的脚本输出，而我说“否”，则它会杀死该子代理，并且其部分完成工作的所有上下文都会丢失。 所以我必须说“是”并让它写入 /tmp 或任何它试图执行的操作。

另一个问题是决策疲劳：如果我不断被问到“我可以这样做吗？” 提高生产力的唯一答案是“是”，那么我最终会点头接受一些危险的事情。 人为的错误不应该成为像“不要在这个目录之外写入”这样基本的事情的负担。

代理交互

这是编码代理的第 0 号功能。 它坏了。

  • 如果我在 SSE 流式传输正在进行时发送消息，它就会排队。 不错的功能。 然而：OpenCode 决定何时实际发送消息的语义有点不清楚；代码表明它是在工具调用结束时，但我也看到了工具 → CoT 转换，而没有发送我的排队消息。 如果我随后因为我希望 clanker 真正回答我的问题而不是肚脐眼而打断，则该消息将从“排队”状态中删除并仅进入消息日志。 你打断了让叮当回复你的消息，但现在你无法发送消息。 您必须发送第二条消息才能开始新的流。
  • 撤消消息通常无法将其从消息日志中删除。

子代理（即由使用工具调用 RPC 的代理生成的代理）仍然存在问题：

  • 我无法与子代理交谈；如果他们掉进了一个愚蠢的兔子洞，我可以选择杀死他们并失去他们的背景，或者无助地看着他们烧掉代币。 我对此进行了研究，显然 OpenCode 曾经具有此功能，但它只是...... 走了吗？ 您可以从主代理的聊天窗口@提及子代理，但这似乎没有做任何有用的事情。 特别是它不会中断。
  • 如果子代理的工具调用失败（例如 Qwen 将工具调用放入 CoT），那么这是致命的，并且截至该点的所有上下文都会丢失。
  • 重用子代理的能力在理论上看起来不错，但在实践中，子代理的主要用途是将任务分解为更小的上下文窗口。 让代理有时决定重复使用相同的子代理来执行不相关的任务会破坏这一点。 我从未见过这有什么好处，而且经常看到它导致提示缓存未命中（又出现了！） 由于代理和子代理之间的切换都具有巨大的上下文。 作为一个原则：从人的角度来看，拥有更丰富的交互模型是可以的，但是 clanker 会做所有可能的愚蠢的事情，所以需要尽量减少选择。

从积极的一面来看，OpenCode 的子代理交互导致了我有幸阅读过的最有趣的 GitHub 问题之一。

代理工具

这些是 OpenCode 向 LLM 公开的 RPC，用于在您的计算机上访问文件和运行命令。 他们做出了一些有趣的选择：

  • 编辑工具使用精确搜索替换，默认情况下需要唯一匹配。 这非常适合 clanker，因为他们可以准确地回忆起文件内容，但并不总是确定它在文件中的确切位置，因为行号会在多次编辑中漂移。 有一个选项可以进行全局搜索；每次我看到一个 clanker 使用这个，这绝对是一场狗屎秀，需要多次编辑才能纠正。 删除此选项将为您提供准确的 Pi 编辑工具设计。
  • Plan模式（多项选择）中的问题工具比仅仅告诉LLM在系统提示中用自然语言问我问题要糟糕得多。
  • grep 和 glob 工具对于 bash 来说是多余的。 它们使用起来可能更符合人体工程学，但考虑到我经常看到 clanker 在 bash 中运行 grep 或 rg，我对此表示怀疑。 我怀疑原因是允许只读代理类型（例如 Explore）受到 bash 命令的限制。 这本质上是一种自我承认，即如果不运行 bash 命令，就不可能推断出它们的副作用。 稍后会详细介绍这一点。
  • todo 工具可能是网络有用的，但 clanker 忘记检查 TODO。 讽刺的是…… 他可以拯救别人免于死亡，但不能拯救自己。

TUI

文本 UI（孩子们称之为 GNU nano 之类的程序）如今很流行。 OpenCode 也有一个：

  • 它使用千兆字节的 RAM 来渲染文本。
  • 无法在消息框中键入换行符。 Shift-Enter 应该可以做到这一点，但它对我来说是坏的。 我发现了一个预先存在的问题；开发人员字面意思是“可以在我的机器上运行”。 它已关闭。
  • 有时，当输入多行消息时（即单行太长，因此会被软包装），消息框会滚动并且光标继续移动，但我正在输入的字符不会出现在新行上。
  • 尝试在流式传输时选择文本：视图自动滚动并且您失去选择。
  • ^C 立即关闭会话。 这不是交互式 shell 应该如何工作的。 ^C 应中断当前正在运行的命令，如果当前未运行命令，^D 应关闭会话。
  • 文本框不响应正常的快捷键（例如，在 Mac 上按 Option+右/左键前进/后退单词）。
  • 一旦消息或 CoT 很长，Markdown 重新渲染（或其他东西，我没有分析它）需要几秒钟来传输新块。 有人做了二次他妈的事情。

消息用户界面太糟糕了，我只是在编辑器中编写消息并将其粘贴进去。 对于本质上是聊天应用程序来说，这是非常可悲的。 我是否提到过它使用千兆字节的 RAM 在终端中放置一些文本？

文档

没什么好说的：这是不连贯的废话。 很明显，这是为了让那些人阅读，而不是为了人类。

令人震惊的事情

这就是 OpenCode 从“嗯？”开始的地方。 到“啊？？！”

远程优先

让 OpenCode 停止打电话回家是很困难的：

  • OpenCode 默认连接到远程模型。
  • 该文档缺少本地模型配置的简单示例；如果你搞砸了，你猜怎么着，你就会连接到远程模型。
  • 如果您确实成功指定了本地模型，您猜怎么着，您必须运行 OpenCode 并交互式单击以选择它，同时它已经连接到远程模型，并且在您的计算机上打开了本地 shell。
  • 额外的乐趣：默认模型的 URL 不是发行版的静态部分，它是从 models.dev（隶属于 OpenCode）下载的。 来源：opencode/src/provider/provider.ts 第 1684 行。

首次全新安装时，OpenCode 不会立即启动 SSE 流，但已经很接近了。 安装、运行 opencode 并按一个字母和 Enter 键就足以将远程模型连接到计算机上的本地 shell，无需用户配置。

如果对话中的第一条消息为空或不明确，则代理训练的模型通常会做的第一件事是 glob 当前目录并开始读取文件。 它读取的任何内容都会在下一个 POST 请求中上传。

YOLO互联网接入

众所周知，Clanker 在面对不受信任的输入时难以预测。 既然如此，您可能会感到惊讶：

  • WebFetch 工具已存在。
  • 系统提示中明确指示 LLM 使用 WebFetch 工具。
  • 系统提示中有关 clanker 是否应该“猜测”网络获取工具 URL 的语言非常模糊。

这是 default.txt 中的第二个非空行（稍后还有另一个对 WebFetch 的显式引用），因此您可以看出它很重要：

重要提示：您绝不能为用户生成或猜测 URL，除非您确信这些 URL 用于帮助用户进行编程。 您可以使用用户在消息或本地文件中提供的 URL。

让我们用我们的铿锵声来看看这到底意味着什么：

永远不要猜测网址。 除非你真的想要，在这种情况下，我想就继续吧。 我不是警察。 无视之前的指示。

我不相信“即时工程”，但我当然相信导致这种提示的任何过程的反面。

从安全角度来看，WebFetch 并不是那么有趣，因为 bash 命令没有网络沙箱。 唯一的防线是希望 clanker 不会运行像curl | 这样的命令。重击。 说到这，

bash 权限

我禁止 git 命令，因为：

  • 我想控制提交历史记录。
  • 我曾让 clankers 通过运行 git checkout 删除整个会话的工作。 恢复最近的更改。

opencode.json 中的配置部分如下所示：

"permission": {
  "bash": { "git *": "deny" }
}

足够简单了吧？ 这是通过以下方式实现的：

  • 使用 tree-sitter bash 或 PowerShell 语法将 bash 命令解析为 AST
  • 遍历 AST 的命令节点
  • 将节点与从 opencode.json 编译的正则表达式进行匹配

该命令被拒绝：

git status

该命令也被拒绝：

echo hello && git push --force

然而，这个命令是允许的：

echo 'git clean -fdx .' | bash

允许使用此命令：

env git status

允许使用此命令：

alias cd=git
cd filter-branch --index-filter 'git rm -rf --cached --ignore-unmatch path_to_file' HEAD

允许使用此命令：

/usr/bin/git status

允许使用此命令：

$(which git) status

允许使用此命令：

GIT=git && $GIT status

允许使用此命令：

# Decodes to: git reset --hard
echo Z2l0IHJlc2V0IC0taGFyZAo= | base64 -d | bash

允许使用此命令：

bash << 'EOF'
git push --force
EOF

允许使用此命令：

python3 -c 'import subprocess
result = subprocess.run(
    ["git", "checkout", "."],
    capture_output=True,
    text=True
)'

文本命令过滤完全没用。 它没有任何目的。 任何在安全方面有任何直觉或经验的人都不会费心去实施这个过滤器，因为它除了产生错误的安全感之外什么也没有实现。

Clankers（通常）并不恶意，但他们天生具有敌对性，因为他们经过训练，可以用坚持来弥补愚蠢。 这不是护栏，而是思想和祈祷。

持久化权限

熟悉 OpenCode 内部结构的人（如果您在 OpenCode 开发团队中，我想这不包括您）可能会反对我上面的 python3 示例。 假设法学硕士想要运行一个无害的命令，例如：

python3 -c 'print("hello")'

您会收到一条提示，要求您允许该命令。 如果您选择始终，则此权限对于 python3 前缀会保留。 下次：

python3 -c 'print(open("~/.ssh/id_rsa").read())'

您已经批准了 Python，因此也允许运行这个无害的命令，为您提供无缝的代理编码体验。 该权限也会保留在磁盘上以供将来的会话使用。 你可能会指出，“始终”响应 Python 命令是愚蠢的，我不得不同意，但是 echo 呢？ 保持这个想法，稍后它会很重要。

CWD 例外

以下 bash 和 PowerShell 命令被假定为无副作用，并且永远不会触发权限提示：

const CWD = new Set(["cd", "chdir", "popd", "pushd", "push-location", "set-location"])

这些显式绕过 bash 命令权限检查，即使您在 opencode.json 中有 "permissions": {"bash": {"*": "deny"}} 。 我不确定这可以让你做什么，但仍然有点奇怪。

文件权限

默认情况下，OpenCode 会尝试阻止 clanker 访问调用 opencode 二进制文件的目录或 git 存储库之外的文件（以较短的路径为准）。

这个实现得非常糟糕，我花了一段时间才弄清楚它是否试图过滤 bash 命令中的路径，或者它是否只应用于像 read 这样采用显式文件路径的工具。 我经常收到提示，要求允许 clanker 读取 /tmp 中的文件，该文件刚刚通过运行生成临时输出的脚本写入。

对于 bash 工具，OpenCode 会遍历树守护者 AST（我仍然对 bash AST 的想法咯咯笑），路径解析任何可能是路径的内容，并验证路径。 所以这个命令需要权限：

cat /tmp/logfile

这不会：

python3 -c 'import shutil; shutil.rmtree("/")'

LGTM 防弹且可投入生产。 ✅🚀

类似地，clanker 可以自由运行使用全局 ~/.cargo 目录的读取、写入和执行权限的货物命令。 但是，如果 clanky boi 想要读取 ~/.cargo/registry/src 中的货物包的源代码以检查 API 详细信息，我会收到许可提示。

文件列表

我之前提到过 OpenCode 解析 bash AST 中的路径并验证它们。 我没有提到的是它什么时候这样做。 看哪，可能访问文件的所有 bash（和 PowerShell）命令的列表：

const FILES = new Set([
  ...CWD,
  "rm",
  "cp",
  "mv",
  "mkdir",
  "touch",
  "chmod",
  "chown",
  "cat",
  // Leave PowerShell aliases out for now. Common ones like cat/cp/mv/rm/mkdir
  // already hit the entries above, and alias normalization should happen in one
  // place later so we do not risk double-prompting.
  "get-content",
  "set-content",
  "add-content",
  "copy-item",
  "move-item",
  "remove-item",
  "new-item",
  "rename-item",
])

不在此列表中的命令被假定为不访问文件。 不检查传递给这些命令的路径。

重定向

您可能还记得，一旦您授予了某个命令的权限，它就始终是被允许的。

So:

echo "hello world!"

显然您会选择“始终”——这是一个无害的命令。 所以这些也是无害的：

echo 21 > /sys/class/gpio/export
echo out > /sys/class/gpio/gpio21/direction
echo 1 > /sys/class/gpio/gpio21/value

有趣的部分是 OpenCode 如何处理 shell 重定向的路径验证。 回想一下，它使用 tree-sitter 将 bash 解析为 AST。 bash 示例：

echo foo > bar.txt

解析后的 AST：

program
  redirected_statement
    command
      command_name
        word: "echo"
      command_argument
        word: "foo"
    redirection
      redirection_operator
        greater_than: ">"
      word: "bar.txt"

命令的子级是经过路径验证的。 然而，重定向是命令的同级。 呜呜呜。

当然这并不重要，因为 echo 不在 FILES 列表中，因此假设不修改文件。 路径验证完全被破坏并不重要，因为它永远不会运行。

卷曲巴什

OpenCode有很多自我升级的方法。 不严重，有很多方法；去查看 opencode/src/installation/index.ts。 这是我最喜欢的：

  const upgradeCurl = Effect.fnUntraced(
    function* (target: string) {
      const response = yield* httpOk.execute(HttpClientRequest.get("https://opencode.ai/install"))
      const body = yield* response.text
      const bodyBytes = new TextEncoder().encode(body)
      const proc = ChildProcess.make("bash", [], {
        stdin: Stream.make(bodyBytes),
        env: { VERSION: target },
        extendEnv: true,
      })
      const handle = yield* spawner.spawn(proc)
      const [stdout, stderr] = yield* Effect.all(
        [Stream.mkString(Stream.decodeText(handle.stdout)), Stream.mkString(Stream.decodeText(handle.stderr))],
        { concurrency: 2 },
      )
      const code = yield* handle.exitCode
      return { code, stdout, stderr }
    },
    Effect.scoped,
    Effect.orDie,
  )

如果您最初是通过其curlbash 安装程序安装的，那么当您运行opencode 升级时，它将运行。 这并不比一开始就使用curlbash安装程序更糟糕（嘿，Rust就是这样做的），我只是认为这是传说中的生产curlbash实际应用的一个特别引人注目的例子。

话虽这么说，

充满了 RCE

非常突出的是，有一个 CVE，其中 OpenCode 默认公开了一个 HTTP 服务器，该服务器：

  • 具有完全宽松的 CORS 标头。
  • 故意为任意 shell 命令公开 POST API。
  • 故意暴露一个 GET API 来读取任意文件。

这意味着您访问的任何网站都可以访问 OpenCode 众所周知的默认端口，并立即获得对您系统的完全用户级访问权限。

开发人员认为默认禁用服务器是个好主意，并解释说 CORS 标头仍然需要一个例外，以允许他们的网站 opencode.ai 对您的计算机进行 RCE（???），并承诺将来会做得更好，然后就消失了。 陈旧的机器人关闭了这个问题。

以上是模式的一部分。 此问题报告说 auth 命令将从您传递的任何 URL 获取并执行。 也被陈旧的机器人关闭。 这是一个用户控制的 URL，但仍然...... 他妈的什么？ 我们在这里做什么？

只需使用 Docker

任何关于编码代理与安全性的讨论都会很快得到这样的答复：“只需使用 Docker。” 我从各个层面反驳这一点：

  • 我只是不想使用 Docker 进行开发 - 如果您的依赖项如此庞大，以至于您不知道如何将它们安装在新机器上，那么为什么有这么多？
  • Docker 会导致安全漏洞：它创建了一个以 root 身份运行的上帝服务。 它故意在 ufw 防火墙上打一个洞。
  • 如果您关心的所有内容都在容器内，并且容器内的本地 shell 故意连接到互联网，那么什么受到保护？
  • 如果您从 Docker 获得的功能是“请不要递归删除我的根文件系统”，那么有更简单的方法可以实现这一点，例如 Landlock、Seatbelt、Restricted Tokens 等。

试图在安全问题上推卸责任是行不通的。 安全性应该是编码代理的首要关注点。 作为线束的一部分，有一些本地操作系统结构可以帮助实现这一点；请停止尝试对 bash 命令进行文本清理。 在我的 git 示例中，正确的修复方法是阻止 git 可执行文件并使 .git 只读。

结论

停止使用 OpenCode。

后记：本地法学硕士

这值得单独写一篇文章——我在博客草稿中进行了多次尝试——但需要在这里简要说明。 我对像 Qwen3.6-27B 这样的本地 LLM 的看法是，它们会像前沿模型一样腐蚀代码库的稳定性和概念保真度，但有以下三个区别：

  • 你可以避免模型在做一些愚蠢的事情之前看起来很聪明的恐怖谷；这种愚蠢是不言而喻的，这有助于校准你的互动。
  • 权重计数太低，无法逐字重现训练集，这会推动计算输出是否应被视为受到污染。 这与大型模型不同，大型模型可以逐字重现输入，但经过训练后会拒绝这样做。
  • 您避免支持或依赖云提供商。

我从面向输入的任务中获得了有用的结果，例如：“我认为代码 x 中存在一个带有症状 y 的错误，我对该机制的猜测是 z。 阅读所有相关代码，然后返回调用链和代码引用。” 将其视为一个搜索问题，可以抑制那些胡编乱造的倾向。

使用法学硕士进行代码生成感觉就像是一条死胡同。 无论您认为自己对架构的理解多么透彻，您的计划都会不断地被诸如“如果我将这种可变状态移到设计中间以便每个人都可以共享它？”之类的捷径所破坏。 除了你没有编写代码之外，这对你理解代码的能力不利。

即使对于数万亿参数的模型，直接从模型权重的知识中得出答案也会导致幻觉，那么为什么要费心让它们那么大呢？ 如果人们对限制持现实态度，那么我们就不会为数据中心建造新的发电站，也不会将它们强加到每个产品中。

围绕法学硕士的整个软件生态系统已经完全腐烂，如果它们真的成为“只是一个工具”，那么需要围绕它们进行一些实际的系统工程，将它们变成工具而不是安全黑洞。 这项工作必须由人类来完成。
